title: Finding 3: Sentiment and Travel Show No Systematic Tilt
text:
Longitudinal meeting sentiment data does not support either lobby’s claim of persistent directional bias. Sentiment oscillates around neutral across all 16 sessions. Member travel destinations span both fishing and tourism zones with no evidence that travel destination influences voting behaviour.

Meeting sessions: 16 (Meeting 1–16; no Meeting 6 — gap in calendar) Overall sentiment trend: oscillates around neutral; no persistent directional shift Travel records: members visited both fishing and tourism zones; no correlation with bias scores

title: Finding 3: Sentiment and Travel Show No Systematic Tilt
text:
Longitudinal meeting sentiment data does not support either lobby’s claim of persistent directional bias. Sentiment oscillates around neutral across all 16 sessions. Member travel destinations span both fishing and tourism zones with no evidence that travel destination influences voting behaviour.

Meeting sessions: 16 (Meeting 1–16; no Meeting 6 — gap in calendar) Overall sentiment trend: oscillates around neutral; no persistent directional shift Travel records: members visited both fishing and tourism zones; no correlation with bias scores

title: Finding 3: Sentiment and Travel Show No Systematic Tilt
text:
Dashboard 3 — Sentiment

Sentiment Over Time

Stacked area chart with dashed sentiment trend line — meeting-level sentiment across 16 sessions. Stacked segments show pro (upper), neutral (middle), against (lower) discussion proportions.
The sentiment over time chart shows: - No persistent trend: sentiment oscillates around neutral; there is no sustained pro-fishing or pro-tourism phase - Gap at Meeting 6: this reflects a meeting that did not take place — a structural calendar feature, not data manipulation - Mid-session dip: a dip around Meetings 8–10 reflects a period of more contentious neutral-topic discussions, not industry bias


Committee Context Comparison — TROUT vs Full Dataset

Diverging bar — TROUT’s apparent committee context compared with the journalist’s full record. Shows how TROUT’s selective inclusion inflates the apparent fishing-lean at the committee level.
This chart makes the dataset comparison concrete: it quantifies what you would conclude using TROUT’s dataset alone versus what the full record shows. The divergence between bars is not a reflection of the committee’s actual behaviour — it is a reflection of TROUT’s editorial choices.


Sentiment Heatmap — Member × Topic

Sentiment heatmap — each cell represents one member × topic combination. Colour encodes average sentiment: green = pro, red = against, grey = neutral/not rated.
The heatmap provides a compact matrix view of all sentiment data simultaneously. Key observations: - No member is consistently pro or consistently against across all topics - Sentiment patterns differ by topic, not by member identity - Members who appear biased in one dataset show neutral sentiment in the full record for the same topics

title: Finding 3: Sentiment and Travel Show No Systematic Tilt
text:
Dashboard 3.1 — Travel

Travel Map — Geographic Trip Distribution

Oceanus map — member travel locations plotted by geographic coordinates. Marker colour encodes industry zone (fishing = blue, tourism = purple). Hover over any marker for city-level visit breakdown.

Tooltip detail — city visits by member (viz-in-tooltip heatmap). Shows which specific cities each member visited and in what frequency.
The travel map shows that member travel destinations are distributed across both fishing and tourism zones of Oceanus. There is no evidence that members who travel more to fishing zones vote more pro-fishing, or vice versa.


Travel Activity Breakdown

Stacked bar chart — travel activity per member by destination zone (fishing = blue, tourism = purple, neutral = green). Sorted by total trip count descending.
The travel activity breakdown quantifies the geographic distribution at member level. No member’s travel is exclusively concentrated in one industry zone — all members visited destinations across both fishing and tourism areas.

title: Finding 3: Sentiment and Travel Show No Systematic Tilt
text:
Key Takeaway
Neither sentiment patterns nor travel behaviour support the lobbies’ accusations of systematic bias. The committee’s behaviour, measured longitudinally across 16 meetings and across geographic travel, shows no directional tilt. The accusations are not supported by behavioural evidence — only by selective dataset construction.

title: Finding 1: Both Datasets Were Curated, Not Collected
text:
Neither FILAH nor TROUT submitted a complete or neutral record. Both datasets are editorial products shaped to support a predetermined conclusion. The lobbies did not simply record fewer meetings — they omitted specific topics and specific members whose records would have weakened their case.

FILAH: 78 records — 45% of the full journalist record TROUT: 54 records — 31% of the full journalist record Journalist: 174 records — 100% complete

title: Finding 1: Both Datasets Were Curated, Not Collected
text:
Neither FILAH nor TROUT submitted a complete or neutral record. Both datasets are editorial products shaped to support a predetermined conclusion. The lobbies did not simply record fewer meetings — they omitted specific topics and specific members whose records would have weakened their case.

FILAH: 78 records — 45% of the full journalist record TROUT: 54 records — 31% of the full journalist record Journalist: 174 records — 100% complete

title: Finding 1: Both Datasets Were Curated, Not Collected
section: Dashboard 1 — What Each Lobby Left Out
text:
Dashboard 1 — What Each Lobby Left Out

Member Participation Across All Three Datasets

Combined member participation stacked bar — all three datasets side by side. Each bar shows the number of discussions a member participated in, colour-coded by industry (fishing = blue, tourism = purple, neutral = green).
The combined participation view exposes that FILAH and TROUT did not just omit topics — they omitted entire members. FILAH recorded only 3 of 6 committee members; TROUT recorded a different 3. Neither lobby recorded the same set.


Missing Discussions

Missing discussions bar — topics each lobby omitted entirely from their dataset. Topics present in the journalist record but with zero records in FILAH or TROUT are shown here.
Both lobbies omitted entire discussion topics. The topics each lobby left out are not random — they are systematically the ones most damaging to their narrative. FILAH omits fishing-heavy discussions; TROUT omits tourism-heavy ones.

title: Finding 1: Both Datasets Were Curated, Not Collected
section: Dashboard 1.1 — How Exclusions Distort the Rankings
text:
Dashboard 1.1 — How Exclusions Distort the Rankings

Slope Graph — Participation Ranking Shifts

Slope graph — member participation ranking shifts from FILAH (left) to TROUT (right), with the journalist record as the centre baseline. Members excluded from a lobby dataset have broken lines.
The slope graph makes the selective inclusion immediately legible as a structural pattern rather than a statistical quirk. Three observations stand out:

Members who are present in the journalist record but absent from a lobby dataset appear as broken lines — the rank simply ends where the lobby stopped recording them.
Lines that cross each other between the FILAH and TROUT columns reveal rank reversals — the same member can be ranked first by one lobby and absent from another.
The journalist column (centre) is the only version that includes all six members, making it the only ranking that reflects the full committee.



Member Participation — FILAH vs TROUT
 
Member participation stacked bars — FILAH dataset (left) and TROUT dataset (right) shown side by side. Use the Dataset Label filter to toggle between individual lobby views.
Placing the two lobby views side by side makes the mirror-image structure of their omissions explicit: FILAH’s three recorded members and TROUT’s three recorded members are almost entirely non-overlapping. Each lobby assembled a version of the committee that supported its predetermined conclusion.

title: Finding 1: Both Datasets Were Curated, Not Collected
text:
Key Takeaway
A lobby that controls which members and which topics it records can produce any bias score it wants — without fabricating a single record. Both lobbies did exactly this. The distortion is not in the data they submitted; it is in the data they withheld.

title: Finding 4: Distortion From Omitted Members, Not Fabricated Data
text:
The lobbies did not fabricate records. The distortion in their datasets comes entirely from selective omission — choosing which members and which discussions to include. The pivotal case is Tante Titan: the committee’s most active member, a strong tourism voice, and the one member excluded from both lobby datasets entirely.

Tante Titan’s record (journalist): 54 total participations — fishing: 3, tourism: 17, neutral: 34 TROUT excluded: Tante Titan (54 records), Carol Limpet (21), Simone Kat (45) — committee appears fishing-leaning in TROUT’s data (fishing=38.9%, tourism=31.5%) FILAH excluded: Tante Titan (54 records), Ed Helpsford (20), Teddy Goldstein (22) — committee appears tourism-leaning in FILAH’s data (tourism=43.6%, fishing=39.7%)

title: Finding 4: Distortion From Omitted Members, Not Fabricated Data
text:
The lobbies did not fabricate records. The distortion in their datasets comes entirely from selective omission — choosing which members and which discussions to include. The pivotal case is Tante Titan: the committee’s most active member, a strong tourism voice, and the one member excluded from both lobby datasets entirely.

Tante Titan’s record (journalist): 54 total participations — fishing: 3, tourism: 17, neutral: 34 TROUT excluded: Tante Titan (54 records), Carol Limpet (21), Simone Kat (45) — committee appears fishing-leaning in TROUT’s data (fishing=38.9%, tourism=31.5%) FILAH excluded: Tante Titan (54 records), Ed Helpsford (20), Teddy Goldstein (22) — committee appears tourism-leaning in FILAH’s data (tourism=43.6%, fishing=39.7%)

title: Finding 4: Distortion From Omitted Members, Not Fabricated Data
text:
Dashboard 4 — Member Overview
This dashboard provides an overview of all six members’ profiles and includes an interactive Person-Picker filter: clicking any member in the influence bubble filters all other charts on this dashboard to that member’s records simultaneously.

Member Influence Bubble

Bubble chart — each bubble represents one COOTEFOO member. X-axis: bias score; Y-axis: total participations. Bubble size encodes total records. Colour encodes bias direction.
The influence bubble provides an overview of all six members’ profiles at a glance:









Member
Bias Direction
Total Participations
Note




Tante Titan
Tourism-leaning
54 (highest)
Excluded from both lobbies


Simone Kat
Balanced

Most active after Tante Titan


Teddy Goldstein
Fishing-leaning

Most polarised fishing voice (−0.32); excluded from FILAH


Carol Limpet
Tourism-leaning
21
Excluded from TROUT


Ed Helpsford
Tourism-leaning
20
Excluded from FILAH


Seal
Fishing-leaning
12 (lowest)
Present in both lobby datasets



Clicking a member in this chart filters all other charts on the dashboard to that member’s records.


Meeting Attendance Heatmap

Attendance heatmap — member (rows) × meeting (columns). Green cell = attended; empty cell = absent. Switch datasets using the filter to compare lobby coverage against the journalist record.
The heatmap shows which members attended which of the 16 committee meetings. Comparing across dataset filters reveals how the lobbies’ selective coverage omits attendance records for their excluded members entirely.


Member Activity Over Time

Line chart — member participation count across meetings 1–16. Each line represents one member. Tante Titan’s consistently high participation is annotated.
Tante Titan did not just attend more meetings — she was consistently the most active participant across all 16 sessions. Her exclusion removes the single most impactful member from both lobby records.

title: Finding 4: Distortion From Omitted Members, Not Fabricated Data
section: Dashboard 4.1 — The Omission Case: FILAH and TROUT
text:
Dashboard 4.1 — The Omission Case: FILAH and TROUT
A Member Filter on this dashboard allows switching between any COOTEFOO member. The Records FILAH Withheld chart, Records TROUT Withheld chart, and Member Topic Profile treemap all update to reflect the selected member. The Member Impact Bar is fixed and shows the overall committee-level effect of all exclusions combined.

Member Impact Bar

Industry share comparison — committee-level industry balance with and without the excluded members. Shows how the lobbies’ omissions shift the apparent committee balance.
Both lobbies excluded members whose records would have undermined their narrative. FILAH excluded Tante Titan, Ed Helpsford, and Teddy Goldstein — critically, Goldstein is the committee’s strongest fishing voice (−0.32), so removing him makes the committee appear more tourism-leaning in FILAH’s data (tourism=43.6%, fishing=39.7%), supporting FILAH’s claim that the committee is pro-tourism. TROUT excluded Tante Titan, Carol Limpet, and Simone Kat — removing all strong tourism voices — making the committee appear fishing-leaning in TROUT’s data (fishing=38.9%, tourism=31.5%), supporting TROUT’s claim that the committee is pro-fishing. The impact bar quantifies exactly how much the committee’s apparent balance shifts when these excluded members are restored.


Records FILAH Withheld

Withheld records bar chart — count of records FILAH withheld for the selected member, broken down by topic. Formula: journalist count − FILAH count per topic. Responds to the Member Filter.
FILAH’s exclusions were wholesale — entire members were removed, not individual topics. The withheld records span all industry categories, confirming that FILAH did not selectively filter by topic type. Switching members reveals which individuals were most impacted by FILAH’s editorial choices.


Records TROUT Withheld

Withheld records bar chart — count of records TROUT withheld for the selected member, broken down by topic. Formula: journalist count − TROUT count per topic. Responds to the Member Filter.
With Tante Titan selected, TROUT withheld 54 records across 8 topics spanning fishing, tourism, and neutral categories — a total, wholesale exclusion. TROUT also excluded Carol Limpet (21 records) and Simone Kat (45 records). Switching members shows the full scale of TROUT’s omissions across all three excluded members.


Member Topic Profile — Treemap

Treemap — selected member’s topic participation from the journalist’s full record. Tile size encodes participation count per topic; colour encodes industry (fishing = blue, tourism = purple, neutral = green). Always uses the Journalist dataset. Responds to the Member Filter.
The treemap updates to show whichever member is selected, revealing each member’s true topic profile from the complete record. With Tante Titan selected, the largest tiles are tourism-related — showing precisely what both lobbies chose to hide. Switching members allows direct comparison of topic profiles across the full committee.

title: Finding 4: Distortion From Omitted Members, Not Fabricated Data
text:
Key Takeaway
Neither FILAH nor TROUT fabricated records. Their bias comes from selecting which records to include. The most egregious case is Tante Titan: 54 records, most active member, strongest tourism voice, excluded from both datasets entirely. Restoring her records to either lobby’s dataset reverses the apparent bias direction.
The distortion is structural. The mechanism is omission. The evidence is clear.

title: Data Transformation
text:
The MC2 VAST Challenge 2025 dataset is structured as a knowledge graph: nodes represent entities (members, discussions, plans, travel destinations) and links represent relationships (participant, about, part_of, travel). Raw data arrived as six CSV pairs — one nodes file and one links file per dataset (FILAH, TROUT, Journalist).
The preparation script mc2_prepare.py (v3) cleans, enriches, and transforms these into flat tables suitable for Tableau and Gephi. All outputs are written to ./tableau_data/.

title: Data Transformation
text:
The MC2 VAST Challenge 2025 dataset is structured as a knowledge graph: nodes represent entities (members, discussions, plans, travel destinations) and links represent relationships (participant, about, part_of, travel). Raw data arrived as six CSV pairs — one nodes file and one links file per dataset (FILAH, TROUT, Journalist).
The preparation script mc2_prepare.py (v3) cleans, enriches, and transforms these into flat tables suitable for Tableau and Gephi. All outputs are written to ./tableau_data/.

title: Data Transformation
text:
Topic–Industry Classification
Fifteen discussion topics were manually classified into three industry categories. This lookup table drives all industry-level aggregations.

5 Fishing topics: Fish Vacuum, Deep Fishing Dock, Low Volume Crane, Seafood Festival, New Crane Lomark
5 Tourism topics: Expanding Tourist Wharf, Marine Life Deck, Heritage Walking Tour, Waterfront Market, Concert
5 Neutral topics: Affordable Housing, Renaming Park Himark, Name Harbor Area, Name Inspection Office, Statue John Smoth

title: Data Transformation
text:
Key Design Decisions

Graph Traversal for Topic Assignment
The primary method for assigning industry to a discussion is graph traversal via about links:
discussion/plan → about → topic_node_id → industry
String inference from node IDs is used only as a fallback for nodes with no about link. This corrects a bug in earlier versions where topic classification relied entirely on substring matching in IDs.


Meeting Number Extraction
Meeting numbers are extracted from part_of links, where source = Meeting_N and target = discussion/plan. This replaces the earlier regex approach, which was error-prone for edge cases.


Bias Score Formula
Bias_Score = (tourism_participations − fishing_participations) / total_participations

Positive = tourism-leaning
Negative = fishing-leaning
Threshold: |score| > 0.15 = directional; within ±0.15 = balanced

title: Data Transformation
text:
Bug Fixes (v1 → v3)








Bug
Symptom
Fix




BUG A
mc2_travel_geo.csv lat/lon always null
Correct join chain: plan → travel link → geo_node → coordinates


BUG B
mc2_travel_geo.csv Is_Member always False
Member found via participant links (source=plan_id, target=member)


BUG C
Non-member orgs leaking into counts
Explicit MEMBERS whitelist with assertion guard


BUG D
Industry inferred from node ID string
Primary: graph traversal (about link); string inference as fallback only


BUG E
NaN Member rows in travel geo
Drop rows where Member is NaN — cross-dataset participant mismatch


BUG F
Goldstein missing deep_fishing_dock topic
Changed inner join to left merge in cross-dataset comparison

text:
Running the Script
Download mc2_prepare.py
python3 mc2_prepare.py
Outputs are written to ./tableau_data/. The script prints row counts and a summary report on completion.

title: Data Transformation
text:
Gephi Network Files
For Finding 2, bipartite member–topic network graphs were built in Gephi using:

Nodes: 6 COOTEFOO members + 15 topic nodes
Edges: Weighted by participation count; colour-coded by industry
Layout: Fruchterman Reingold

Three separate network graphs were produced — one per dataset (FILAH, TROUT, Journalist) — to make the structural differences in what each lobby chose to record visually apparent.






filah







journalist







trout





Three Gephi bipartite networks side-by-side: FILAH (left), Journalist (centre), TROUT (right)

title: About
text:
Data Sources
All data originates from the VAST Challenge 2025 Mini-Challenge 2 dataset:

mc2_FILAH_nodes.csv / mc2_FILAH_links.csv
mc2_TROUT_nodes.csv / mc2_TROUT_links.csv

The data is fictional and created for the purposes of the VAST Challenge educational competition.

title: Conclusion & Future Work
text:
Both datasets are curated, not collected. FILAH captured 78 of 174 records (45%) and systematically over-represents fishing topics; TROUT captured 54 (31%) and systematically over-represents tourism topics. Both lobbies also selectively omitted members: FILAH recorded only 3 of 6 members; TROUT recorded a different 3. The missing discussions bar and member participation slope chart make this selective inclusion visually explicit.
Verdict: Both datasets exhibit clear, measurable selection bias in topic coverage and member inclusion.




No. Using the journalist’s complete record of 174 discussions across 16 meetings, no member’s bias score is statistically extreme. Three members lean slightly toward fishing; three lean slightly toward tourism. The confidence intervals confirm that individual deviations are within normal variance. The scatter/quadrant chart shows the committee is evenly split.
Verdict: The full COOTEFOO committee is balanced. Bias is not a property of the committee.




The same six members appear biased in different directions depending solely on which dataset is read. FILAH’s version inflates the apparent fishing-lean by 40–60%; TROUT’s version inflates the apparent tourism-lean by the same amount. Switching to the journalist’s dataset eliminates apparent bias for all six members. Sentiment and travel data from the full record do not support either lobby’s claim of persistent systematic bias.
Verdict: Bias scores are a property of the dataset, not the committee. Both lobby narratives are undermined by the full record.




Six member profiles were built using the Person-Picker interactive dashboard:



Member
Bias Direction
Total Participations (Journalist)




Tante Titan
Tourism-leaning
54 (most active)


Simone Kat
Balanced
45


Teddy Goldstein
Fishing-leaning
22


Carol Limpet
Tourism-leaning
21


Ed Helpsford
Tourism-leaning
20


Seal
Fishing-leaning
12 (fewest)



Teddy Goldstein is the most polarised fishing voice (bias score −0.32). Tante Titan is the most active member and the strongest tourism voice. Simone Kat, despite high fishing participation (20), is balanced overall due to near-equal tourism participation (19). The Person-Picker filter on Dashboard 4 allows interactive exploration of each member’s attendance, topic breakdown, and activity over time.




Member: Tante Titan
TROUT accused Tante Titan of being a fishing advocate — yet their own dataset contains zero of her 54 records. In the journalist’s full record, she is the committee’s strongest tourism voice (tourism=17, fishing=3, total=54). Adding her back to the record reverses the apparent fishing-lean that TROUT’s dataset implies. TROUT’s accusation against Tante Titan is directly contradicted by the data they chose to withhold.
Understanding before full data: Tante Titan appears absent or irrelevant. Understanding after full data: Tante Titan is the most active member and TROUT’s principal counterevidence.




TROUT withheld 54 records for Tante Titan across 8 topics. The withheld topics span all three industry categories — fishing, tourism, and neutral — indicating a wholesale exclusion rather than selective topic filtering. The “Records TROUT Withheld for Tante Titan” bar chart (Dashboard 4.1) shows the per-topic breakdown.




FILAH excluded Tante Titan (+0.26), Ed Helpsford (+0.25), and Teddy Goldstein (−0.32). The most consequential exclusion is Teddy Goldstein — the committee’s strongest fishing voice. By removing him, FILAH eliminated the member most likely to undermine its claim that the committee is pro-tourism. With these three excluded, FILAH’s remaining members (Carol Limpet, Simone Kat, Seal) make the committee appear tourism-leaning (tourism=43.6%, fishing=39.7%) — directly supporting FILAH’s accusation. The Member Impact Bar quantifies how much the committee’s apparent balance shifts when the excluded members are restored.




FILAH’s data shows the committee as slightly tourism-leaning (tourism=43.6%, fishing=39.7%) — a picture engineered by excluding Teddy Goldstein, the strongest fishing voice, along with two other members. In the full journalist record, the industry breakdown shifts to fishing=28.2%, tourism=36.8%, neutral=35.1%, and by member bias scores the committee has two fishing-leaning members, three tourism-leaning, and one balanced. FILAH’s claim of a pro-tourism committee is a product of selective omission — specifically the removal of Goldstein — not an objective property of the committee.

Both datasets are curated, not collected. The missing discussions bar and member participation slope chart make this selective inclusion visually explicit.
Verdict: Both datasets exhibit clear, measurable selection bias in topic coverage and member inclusion.




No. Using the journalist’s complete record of 174 discussions across 16 meetings, no member’s bias score is statistically extreme. Three members lean slightly toward fishing; three lean slightly toward tourism. The confidence intervals confirm that individual deviations are within normal variance. The scatter/quadrant chart shows the committee is evenly split.
Verdict: The full COOTEFOO committee is balanced. Bias is not a property of the committee.

title: Conclusion & Future Work
text:
Overall Conclusion






Important



Neither lobby’s accusation is supported by the full dataset.
The COOTEFOO committee, when assessed using the journalist’s complete record, is balanced. Both FILAH and TROUT constructed their datasets to create the appearance of bias — not by fabricating records, but by selectively omitting members and discussions that contradicted their narrative. The key mechanism in both cases is the same: exclude the members whose records weaken your case.

title: Conclusion & Future Work
text:
Limitations
Data completeness: We treat the journalist dataset as the ground truth. If the journalist also made selective choices, our baseline may be imperfect. The challenge documentation presents the journalist record as comprehensive, but this cannot be independently verified.
Bias score formula: (tourism − fishing) / total is one of many possible bias metrics. It treats all participations equally regardless of topic weight, sentiment, or outcome. Alternative formulations (e.g., sentiment-weighted, vote-outcome-weighted) might produce different scores.
Missing meetings: Meeting 6 has no data. Whether this reflects a cancelled meeting, a recording gap, or a data extraction error is unknown. We treat it as a structural calendar feature.
Gephi layout instability: ForceAtlas2 network layouts are non-deterministic. The visual structure of the bipartite networks varies slightly between runs, though the structural findings (which nodes are connected) are deterministic.

title: Conclusion & Future Work
text:
Future Work
Temporal analysis: Sentiment was analysed cross-sectionally per meeting. A longitudinal regression controlling for meeting date and topic type could yield more robust evidence about directional trends.
Outlier detection: A formal statistical test (e.g., bootstrap confidence intervals, Bayesian inference) could quantify the probability that each member’s observed bias score could arise by chance.
Network centrality: Gephi’s bipartite networks could be extended with centrality metrics (betweenness, eigenvector) to identify structurally influential members beyond raw participation counts.
Cross-validation with outcomes: If committee voting records or policy outcomes were available, they could be used to validate whether observed bias scores translate into differential policy impact.

Original Poster
Submitted and presented at the IS428 class poster session on 13 April 2026.

title: Project Poster
text:
Poster Session Feedback
The poster was presented at the IS428 class session on 13 April 2026. Four groups provided written evaluations.
What worked well:

The core message — bias comes from selective omission, not fabricated data — was clearly communicated and consistently understood across all evaluators
Key insights panels were highlighted as an effective way to draw quick conclusions before engaging with the group
The lollipop/bias bar chart was noted for giving a clear picture of each member’s sentiment range at a glance
Good variety of chart types and effective use of colour and legends

Areas for improvement:

A brief context introduction was missing — several evaluators needed to ask for background before they could interpret the poster
The sentiment heatmap was difficult to read due to many null values; a cleaner chart or alternative visual was suggested
Some charts were too small to read comfortably at poster size
Colour palette was perceived as inconsistent across the project
Axis titles on the sentiment chart were not renamed from defaults

title: Detecting Bias in COOTEFOO’s Oversight Data
text:
The Commission on Overseeing the Economic Future of Oceanus (COOTEFOO) sits at the centre of a public controversy. Two advocacy groups — FILAH (fishing lobby) and TROUT (tourism lobby) — have each released datasets claiming to prove that the committee is biased against their industry. A journalist independently obtained a more complete record of all committee meetings.
This project uses visual analytics to audit all three datasets and determine who, if anyone, is telling the truth.

title: Detecting Bias in COOTEFOO’s Oversight Data
text:
The Commission on Overseeing the Economic Future of Oceanus (COOTEFOO) sits at the centre of a public controversy. Two advocacy groups — FILAH (fishing lobby) and TROUT (tourism lobby) — have each released datasets claiming to prove that the committee is biased against their industry. A journalist independently obtained a more complete record of all committee meetings.
This project uses visual analytics to audit all three datasets and determine who, if anyone, is telling the truth.

title: Detecting Bias in COOTEFOO’s Oversight Data
text:
The Three Datasets










Dataset
Source
Records
Share of Full Record




FILAH
Fishing lobby (advocacy)
78
45%


TROUT
Tourism lobby (advocacy)
54
31%


Journalist
Independent (full record)
174
100%




The journalist dataset is the authoritative baseline. FILAH captured 78 of 174 records; TROUT captured only 54.

title: Detecting Bias in COOTEFOO’s Oversight Data
text:
The Four Findings






Finding 1 — Both Datasets Were Curated, Not Collected FILAH and TROUT each systematically over-represent their preferred industry and exclude discussions that undermine their case.









Finding 2 — The Full Committee Is Balanced Using the journalist’s complete record, no member’s bias score is statistically extreme. Two members lean toward fishing, three toward tourism, and one is balanced.









Finding 3 — Sentiment and Travel Show No Systematic Tilt Meeting sentiment oscillates around neutral across all 16 sessions. Member travel destinations span both fishing and tourism zones.









Finding 4 — The Distortion Comes from Omitted Members, Not Fabricated Data Tante Titan — the committee’s most active member and a strong tourism voice — was excluded from both lobby datasets entirely. Restoring her records reverses TROUT’s apparent fishing-lean finding.

title: Prototypes & Design Evolution
text: This page documents the design process behind the final dashboards — from initial per-task sketches through to the refined 9-dashboard finding-based layout. Each iteration addressed specific clarity or coverage issues identified during design review.

title: Prototypes & Design Evolution
text: This page documents the design process behind the final dashboards — from initial per-task sketches through to the refined 9-dashboard finding-based layout. Each iteration addressed specific clarity or coverage issues identified during design review.

title: Prototypes & Design Evolution
text:
Design Principles
The dashboards follow five core principles:

One conclusion per view — each dashboard communicates exactly one finding
Dataset toggle — the shared Dataset_Label filter lets users switch between FILAH, TROUT, and Journalist across all charts simultaneously
Consistent colour palette — fishing = blue, tourism = purple, neutral = green — never deviated
No repeated charts — every chart appears on exactly one dashboard
Fixed 1200 × 800px — tiled layout, no floating objects

title: Prototypes & Design Evolution
text:
Version 1 — Per-Task Sketches
The first version was a hand-drawn sketch for each MC2 task question. One sketch per task, designed independently before any dashboard structure was decided.

title: Prototypes & Design Evolution
text:
Version 2 — Finding-Based Layout (Final)
After reviewing the per-task sketches, charts serving the same narrative arc were grouped into shared dashboards. Tasks were not kept as individual dashboards — instead, the structure was reorganised around four findings plus an overview entry point.
Key decisions made at this stage:

Tasks 4a and 4b were co-located because the member omission evidence and the Tante Titan profile tell one continuous story
Dashboard 2.1 was added as a bridge to make the mechanism of bias manipulation explicit before moving to the full-record findings
Dashboard 3 was split into D3 (sentiment) and D3.1 (travel) to avoid chart density
Dashboard 4 was split into D4 (member overview) and D4.1 (omission case) for the same reason

title: Prototypes & Design Evolution
text:
Colour System
The colour palette was fixed at the start of design to avoid visual inconsistency.

Fishing: blue Tourism: purple Neutral: green

title: Prototypes & Design Evolution
text:
Chart Type Selection
Each chart type was chosen to support a specific analytical task:








Chart Type
Used For
Reason




100% stacked bar
Industry share per dataset
Shows proportional split; comparison across 3 groups


Diverging bar (lollipop)
Bias scores per member
Centred on zero; direction and magnitude visible


Slope graph
Participation rank shifts
Highlights rank reversals caused by selective inclusion


Scatter/quadrant
Fishing vs tourism participation
Shows cluster structure; identifies balanced vs polarised members


Heatmap (member × topic)
Sentiment patterns
Compact grid; colour encodes sentiment direction


Treemap
Proportional coverage
Size = volume; nesting = member → industry hierarchy


Bubble chart
Influence × bias
Two-dimension encoding: bias score and total activity


Stacked area + line
Sentiment over time
Longitudinal trend; meeting gap visible as axis break


Geographic map
Member travel
Spatial distribution of travel activity


Attendance heatmap
Meeting presence
Matrix view; absences visible as blank cells

title: User Guide
text:
This guide provides step-by-step instructions for navigating the interactive Tableau storyboard. The application consists of 9 dashboards, each communicating one finding. This guide covers the controls and interactive features available on each dashboard.
To view the storyboard, visit the Final Storyboard page.

title: User Guide
text:
This guide provides step-by-step instructions for navigating the interactive Tableau storyboard. The application consists of 9 dashboards, each communicating one finding. This guide covers the controls and interactive features available on each dashboard.
To view the storyboard, visit the Final Storyboard page.

title: User Guide
text:
Navigating the Storyboard
This dashboard serves as the entry point to the application, providing an overview of all three datasets and orienting the viewer before the four findings are presented.


Story Point Navigation — Users can move between the 9 dashboards using the navigation controls at the top of the storyboard.

Users can click the left/right arrow buttons to move to the previous or next dashboard sequentially.
Users can click any labelled tab directly to jump to a specific dashboard.

title: User Guide
section: Dashboard 0 — Overview: Detecting Bias in COOTEFOO
text:
Dashboard 0 — Overview: Detecting Bias in COOTEFOO
This dashboard serves as the entry point to the application. It presents all three datasets side by side — FILAH, TROUT, and the Journalist — so that the viewer can immediately see how differently the same committee is represented depending on which dataset is read.


Industry Share Per Dataset (top) — Displays the proportion of fishing, tourism, and neutral discussions recorded by each of the three datasets.

Users can hover over any bar segment to see the exact count and percentage for that industry category.
The three bars (FILAH, TROUT, Journalist) are always shown together — no filter is applied to this chart.

Record Count Per Dataset (bottom left) — Displays the total number of discussion records in each dataset.

Users can hover over a bar to see the exact record count.

Diverging Bias Bar: Member Bias Scores (bottom right) — Displays each member’s bias score from the Journalist dataset. Members extending left are fishing-leaning (blue); members extending right are tourism-leaning (purple).

Users can hover over any lollipop to see the member’s name, exact bias score, and bias direction label.

Dataset Label Filter — Users can select a dataset using the filter panel to change which dataset is reflected in the bias bar.

Users can select filah, trout, or journalist.
Changing the filter updates the bias bar chart to reflect that dataset’s member scores.

title: User Guide
section: Dashboard 1 — Finding 1: What Each Lobby Left Out
text:
Dashboard 1 — Finding 1: What Each Lobby Left Out
This dashboard shows that the lobbies’ omissions are not accidental — they systematically excluded the topics and members most damaging to their case.


Member Participation Stacked Bar (top) — Shows the total number of discussions each member participated in, broken down by industry (fishing / tourism / neutral), for all three datasets side by side.

Members excluded from the selected dataset will not appear in the chart.
Users can hover over a bar segment to see the member’s name, industry category, and participation count.

Missing Discussions Bar (bottom) — Shows which topics each lobby omitted entirely from their dataset.

Users can hover over a bar to see the topic name, industry category, and number of records omitted.
Topics are sorted by number of omitted records descending.

title: User Guide
section: Dashboard 1.1 — Finding 1 (Continued): How Exclusions Distort the Rankings
text:
Dashboard 1.1 — Finding 1 (Continued): How Exclusions Distort the Rankings
This dashboard shows the structural consequence of the lobbies’ member exclusions: the same six committee members produce entirely different rankings depending on which lobby’s dataset you read.


Slope Graph — Shows how each member’s participation ranking shifts from the FILAH dataset (left) to the TROUT dataset (right), with the Journalist record as the centre baseline.

Members excluded from a lobby dataset have a broken line — the line ends at the last dataset that includes them.
Users can hover over any point to see the member’s name, dataset, and rank.
Lines that cross each other indicate rank reversals caused by selective inclusion.

Member Participation — FILAH vs TROUT — Places the FILAH-only and TROUT-only participation bars side by side for direct comparison.

Users can hover over a bar segment to see the member’s name, industry category, and participation count.
The near-complete absence of overlap between the two lobbies’ recorded members is immediately visible.

title: User Guide
section: Dashboard 2 — Finding 2: The Full Committee Is Balanced
text:
Dashboard 2 — Finding 2: The Full Committee Is Balanced
This dashboard uses the journalist’s complete record to assess whether the COOTEFOO committee is genuinely biased. Four complementary charts are used to triangulate the finding.


Diverging Bias Bar: Member Bias Scores (top left) — Shows each member’s bias score using the journalist dataset. The zero line is the balance point.

Users can hover over any lollipop to see the member’s name, bias score, and direction label.
The chart is fixed to the Journalist dataset — it always reflects the full record.

Confidence Interval Chart (top right) — Shows the statistical uncertainty around each member’s bias score.

Users can hover over any error bar to see the member’s bias score, confidence interval bounds, and total participation count.
Members with fewer participations have wider intervals — their scores are less reliable.

Fishing vs Tourism Participation Quadrant (bottom left) — Plots each member by fishing participation (x-axis) vs tourism participation (y-axis).

Users can hover over any point to see the member’s name and exact fishing/tourism counts.
The quadrant dividers show the average fishing and tourism participation levels — points in the upper-left lean fishing; lower-right lean tourism.

Treemap (bottom right) — Shows proportional coverage by member and industry. Tile size encodes total participation count.

Users can hover over a tile to see the member name, industry category, and record count.
The largest tile (Tante Titan) is immediately visible — her dominance of the full record is the basis for Finding 4.

title: User Guide
section: Dashboard 2.1 — Bridge: How Selective Reporting Creates a False Narrative
text:
Dashboard 2.1 — Bridge: How Selective Reporting Creates a False Narrative
This dashboard places all three datasets side by side so that the mechanism of bias distortion is visible at a glance. The same six members appear with different bias scores depending solely on which dataset is used.


Side-by-Side Bias Bars: All Three Datasets (top) — Displays the member bias scores from FILAH, Journalist, and TROUT in three adjacent panels.

All three datasets are shown simultaneously — the dataset filter is not used on this dashboard.
Users can hover over any lollipop to see the member’s name, dataset, and bias score.
The same member can be compared across all three panels by reading horizontally.

Topic Breakdown Stacked Bar (bottom left) — Shows how each member distributes their participation across industry topics using the journalist record.

Users can hover over any segment to see the member, topic, and count.
Bars are sorted by total participation descending.

Pro/Against Stacked Bar (bottom right) — Shows each member’s vote direction (pro, against, neutral) across discussions.

Users can hover over any segment to see the member, sentiment label, and count.

title: User Guide
text:
Dashboard 3 — Finding 3: Sentiment
This dashboard examines whether meeting sentiment shows any persistent directional bias across the 16 committee sessions.


Sentiment Over Time (top, full width) — Shows meeting-level sentiment across all 16 sessions as a stacked area chart with a trend line overlay.

Users can hover over any point on the trend line to see the meeting number and average sentiment score.
The gap between Meeting 5 and Meeting 7 reflects a session that was not held — this is a structural calendar feature, not a data gap.

Committee Context Comparison (bottom left) — Compares TROUT’s apparent committee-level industry balance with the journalist’s full record.

Users can hover over bars to see the dataset, industry category, and count.

Sentiment Heatmap: Member × Topic (bottom right) — Displays average sentiment for each member × topic combination as a colour-coded grid.

Users can hover over any cell to see the member name, topic, average sentiment score, and sentiment label.
Green cells = pro; red cells = against; grey cells = neutral or not rated.
Users can use the Dataset Label filter to compare sentiment grids across datasets.

title: User Guide
text:
Dashboard 3.1 — Finding 3 (Continued): Travel
This dashboard examines whether member travel destinations show any geographic concentration in fishing or tourism zones that could indicate behavioural bias.


Travel Map (main) — Shows COOTEFOO member travel destinations on a map of Oceanus. Markers are colour-coded by industry zone.

Users can hover over any marker to open a tooltip heatmap showing city-level visit breakdowns by member.
Users can zoom in and out, and reset the map view by clicking the home icon.
Users can use the Dataset Label filter to view only the travel records captured by a specific dataset.

Travel Activity Breakdown (supporting) — Shows total travel activity per member broken down by destination zone (fishing / tourism / neutral).

Users can hover over any bar segment to see the member name, destination zone, and trip count.
Bars are sorted by total trip count descending.

title: User Guide
text:
Dashboard 4 — Finding 4: Member Overview
This dashboard provides an overview of all six members’ profiles. Clicking any member in the Influence Bubble activates the Person-Picker filter, updating all other charts on this dashboard to that member’s records.


Member Influence Bubble (top left) — Plots each member by bias score (x-axis) and total participations (y-axis). Bubble size encodes total record count.

Users can click on any bubble to activate the Person-Picker filter — all other charts on this dashboard will update to show only that member’s records.
Users can click the same bubble again (or press Esc) to clear the filter and return to the full committee view.
Users can hover over any bubble to see the member’s name, bias score, bias direction, and total participations.

Meeting Attendance Heatmap (top right) — Shows which members attended which of the 16 meetings. Green cells = attended; blank cells = absent.

Users can hover over any cell to see the member name, meeting number, and discussion count for that session.
When the Person-Picker is active, only the selected member’s row is highlighted.

Member Activity Over Time (bottom) — Shows each member’s participation count across all 16 meetings as a line chart.

Users can hover over any point to see the member, meeting number, and discussion count.
When the Person-Picker is active, the selected member’s line is highlighted.

title: User Guide
text:
Dashboard 4.1 — Finding 4 (Continued): The Omission Case
This dashboard presents the full evidence for the lobbies’ selective member exclusions. A Member Filter lets users switch between members — the two withheld-record charts and the topic profile treemap all update to reflect the selected member. Tante Titan is selected by default as the pivotal case.


Member Impact Bar (top left) — Compares the committee’s apparent industry balance with and without the excluded members. Quantifies how far the lobbies’ omissions shift the committee’s apparent balance.

Users can hover over the bars to see the exact industry share percentages with and without the excluded members.
This chart covers all excluded members collectively and does not respond to the Member Filter.

Member Filter — Users can select any COOTEFOO member using the filter to explore that member’s withheld records and topic profile.

Changing the selected member updates the Records FILAH Withheld chart, the Records TROUT Withheld chart, and the Member Topic Profile treemap simultaneously.
Members who were not excluded by a lobby will show zero withheld records in that lobby’s chart.

Records FILAH Withheld (top middle) — Shows the per-topic count of records that FILAH omitted for the selected member. Formula: Journalist count − FILAH count per topic.

Users can hover over any bar to see the topic name, industry category, and number of withheld records.
Responds to the Member Filter — switching members updates this chart.
Bars are sorted by number of withheld records descending.

Records TROUT Withheld (top right) — Shows the per-topic count of records that TROUT omitted for the selected member. Formula: Journalist count − TROUT count per topic.

Users can hover over any bar to see the topic name, industry category, and number of withheld records.
Responds to the Member Filter — switching members updates this chart.
Members not excluded by TROUT will show zero withheld records.

Member Topic Profile — Treemap (bottom, full width) — Shows the selected member’s full topic participation from the journalist record. Tile size encodes participation count; colour encodes industry (fishing = blue, tourism = purple, neutral = green).

Responds to the Member Filter — switching members updates the treemap to reflect that member’s journalist record.
This chart always uses the Journalist dataset — it does not respond to the Dataset Label filter.
Users can hover over any tile to see the topic name, industry category, and participation count.

For a detailed walkthrough of each dashboard and how to use the interactive features, see the User Guide.

For a detailed walkthrough of each dashboard and how to use the interactive features, see the User Guide.

Dashboard Overview







Dashboard

Dashboard 1 — Finding 1

title: Project Proposal: The COOTEFOO Conflict
text:
The island nation of Oceanus is undergoing a major economic transition from a traditional fishing economy to a rapidly expanding tourism sector. While tourism growth presents new economic opportunities, it has also created tensions among stakeholders who fear unequal policy treatment.
To guide the country’s economic future, the government established the Commission on Overseeing the Economic Future of Oceanus (COOTEFOO). Although the commission does not directly set policy, its recommendations carry substantial influence over economic planning and public spending.
Recently, two influential advocacy groups have released conflicting datasets alleging bias in COOTEFOO’s decision-making:

FILAH claims that tourism interests are dominating government attention.


TROUT argues that traditional fishing interests are being protected unfairly.

Both groups have published selective datasets to support their claims. However, a journalist has obtained a more complete dataset containing the full set of meetings, travel records, and relationships among stakeholders.
The existence of three competing datasets has created public confusion and reduced trust in government oversight. Citizens cannot easily determine which narrative is accurate.
This project therefore seeks to apply visual analytics and knowledge graph analysis to audit these datasets and determine whether bias exists in the narratives presented by each advocacy group.

title: Project Proposal: The COOTEFOO Conflict
text:
The island nation of Oceanus is undergoing a major economic transition from a traditional fishing economy to a rapidly expanding tourism sector. While tourism growth presents new economic opportunities, it has also created tensions among stakeholders who fear unequal policy treatment.
To guide the country’s economic future, the government established the Commission on Overseeing the Economic Future of Oceanus (COOTEFOO). Although the commission does not directly set policy, its recommendations carry substantial influence over economic planning and public spending.
Recently, two influential advocacy groups have released conflicting datasets alleging bias in COOTEFOO’s decision-making:

FILAH claims that tourism interests are dominating government attention.


TROUT argues that traditional fishing interests are being protected unfairly.

Both groups have published selective datasets to support their claims. However, a journalist has obtained a more complete dataset containing the full set of meetings, travel records, and relationships among stakeholders.
The existence of three competing datasets has created public confusion and reduced trust in government oversight. Citizens cannot easily determine which narrative is accurate.
This project therefore seeks to apply visual analytics and knowledge graph analysis to audit these datasets and determine whether bias exists in the narratives presented by each advocacy group.

title: Project Proposal: The COOTEFOO Conflict
text:
Project Objectives
Identifying Data Gaps
To what extent do the datasets released by FILAH and TROUT differ from the comprehensive records obtained by the journalist? Which specific meetings, discussions, or travel records were omitted, and how might these omissions influence the narratives presented by each advocacy group?
Evaluating Individual Behavioral Bias
By profiling individual COOTEFOO members, can visual analysis reveal significant differences in perceived behavior when comparing the advocacy datasets to the complete knowledge graph?
Understanding Institutional Patterns
When examining the full dataset, does COOTEFOO as a committee exhibit systematic bias toward either the fishing or tourism sectors?
How Does Data Framing Influence Public Perception?
When users switch between datasets from different advocacy groups, how does the visual narrative change?

title: Project Proposal: The COOTEFOO Conflict
text:
Scope of Work
The project will involve four main stages of visual analytics development.

Multi-Source Data Wrangling
Data processing will be conducted using Gephi and Tableau for visualisation. This stage ensures consistency in node identifiers, link relationships, timestamps, and geographic information, enabling reliable comparison across datasets.
Key tasks include: - Cleaning and standardizing node attributes - Aligning node IDs and link IDs across datasets - Identifying duplicate or missing entities - Creating a unified schema for comparative analysis


Comparative Link Analysis
Once the data is standardized, network visualizations will be created to identify missing or manipulated connections. Key techniques include: - Network graphs showing relationships between COOTEFOO members and stakeholders - Highlighting links that appear in the journalist dataset but are absent in FILAH or TROUT datasets - Filtering tools to isolate specific relationship types (meetings, travel, collaborations)


Spatiotemporal Behavioral Mapping
Using geographic coordinates and timestamps, the movements and activities of COOTEFOO members will be mapped across time and location. This analysis will highlight clusters of activity associated with fishing or tourism zones, providing insight into real-world behavioral patterns.
Visual techniques will include: - Geographic maps showing travel locations - Timeline charts displaying meeting frequency over time - Cluster analysis identifying concentrated visits to fishing or tourism regions


Reporting and Visualization Summary
Key findings regarding sampling bias, omitted records, and institutional behavior will be summarized through visual dashboards and a final analytical report.
Dataset Perspective Toggle
Users can switch between: - FILAH dataset - TROUT dataset - Journalist ground truth dataset
Bias Indicators
Metrics such as missing link counts, stakeholder interaction frequency, and sector engagement ratios will provide quantifiable evidence of sampling bias.
Member Profiles
Interactive panels will allow users to select individual COOTEFOO members and view meeting networks, travel patterns, and sector engagement.

title: Project Proposal: The COOTEFOO Conflict
text:
Expected Outcomes
The final output will consist of:
An Interactive Tableau Visual Analytics Application
Allowing users to explore relationships, travel patterns, and dataset differences.
Bias Detection Analysis
Identification of missing records or selective data presentation by advocacy groups.
Evidence-Based Findings
A clear assessment of whether the accusations made by FILAH or TROUT are supported by the full dataset.
Final Poster and Analytical Report
Summarizing the methodology, visual findings, and implications for government transparency.

title: Project Proposal: The COOTEFOO Conflict
text:
Significance of the Project
This project demonstrates how visual analytics can support transparency, accountability, and evidence-based decision making. By revealing how data can be selectively presented to support competing narratives, the project highlights the importance of complete datasets and transparent analysis in public policy debates.
For the citizens of Oceanus, the analysis may help restore confidence in the oversight process and clarify the role of COOTEFOO in guiding the nation’s economic transition.

title: Project Proposal: The COOTEFOO Conflict
text: Project Schedule

title: Finding 2: The Full Committee Is Balanced
text:
Using the journalist’s complete record, no member’s bias score is statistically extreme. Two members lean toward fishing, three toward tourism, and one is balanced — the committee is not captured by either industry. The confidence intervals confirm that individual deviations are within normal variance.

Bias score range (journalist): −0.32 (Teddy Goldstein) to +0.29 (Carol Limpet) Threshold: |bias| > 0.15 = directional; within ±0.15 = balanced Net committee bias: near zero — fishing-leaning and tourism-leaning members broadly cancel out

title: Finding 2: The Full Committee Is Balanced
text:
Using the journalist’s complete record, no member’s bias score is statistically extreme. Two members lean toward fishing, three toward tourism, and one is balanced — the committee is not captured by either industry. The confidence intervals confirm that individual deviations are within normal variance.

Bias score range (journalist): −0.32 (Teddy Goldstein) to +0.29 (Carol Limpet) Threshold: |bias| > 0.15 = directional; within ±0.15 = balanced Net committee bias: near zero — fishing-leaning and tourism-leaning members broadly cancel out

title: Finding 2: The Full Committee Is Balanced
text:
Dashboard 2 — Committee Balance (Full Record)

Member Bias Scores — Journalist Dataset

Diverging lollipop bar — Member Bias Scores using the journalist’s full record. Fishing-leaning members extend left (blue); tourism-leaning members extend right (purple). Zero line is the balance point.
The lollipop chart shows each member’s bias score centred on zero. The visual immediately communicates that the committee splits evenly: some members lean fishing, some lean tourism, with no extreme outliers when the full record is used.


Confidence Intervals

Confidence interval chart — statistical significance of individual bias scores. Error bars represent the uncertainty around each member’s score given their total participation count.
The confidence intervals confirm that no member’s score deviates significantly from zero in a statistically meaningful sense. Members with fewer total participations have wider intervals — their scores are less reliable. This chart prevents the lollipop from being over-interpreted.


Fishing vs Tourism Participation Quadrant

Scatter/quadrant chart — each member plotted by fishing participation (x-axis) vs tourism participation (y-axis). Quadrant lines divide fishing-dominant from tourism-dominant regions.
The scatter quadrant shows the committee’s structure: members with high tourism and low fishing participation fall in the upper-left (tourism-dominant) quadrant; members with high fishing and low tourism fall in the lower-right (fishing-dominant) quadrant. No member occupies an extreme corner. This confirms the finding visually without relying on the bias score formula.


Treemap — Proportional Coverage

Treemap — each tile represents one member × industry combination. Tile size encodes total participation count; colour encodes industry.
The treemap makes one fact immediately obvious: Tante Titan is the largest tile. She dominates total participation and is tourism-leaning. Her exclusion from both lobby datasets was not accidental — removing her record materially alters the committee’s apparent industry balance.

title: Finding 2: The Full Committee Is Balanced
section: Dashboard 2.1 — How Selective Reporting Creates a False Narrative
text:
Dashboard 2.1 — How Selective Reporting Creates a False Narrative
This bridge dashboard shows the same six members through three different lenses: FILAH’s version, the journalist’s version, and TROUT’s version. The finding is structural: member behaviour does not change, only the framing does.

Side-by-Side Bias Bars — All Three Datasets

Three bias bar charts side by side — FILAH (left), Journalist (centre), TROUT (right). The same member can look fishing-biased in one and tourism-biased in another.
This is the most powerful chart in the project. It shows that FILAH’s version makes the committee appear tourism-leaning — supporting FILAH’s claim that the committee is biased against fishing. TROUT’s version makes the committee appear fishing-leaning — supporting TROUT’s claim that the committee is biased against tourism. The journalist’s version shows a balanced committee that neither lobby’s accusation describes.


Topic Breakdown Stacked Bar — Per Member

Topic breakdown stacked bar — how each member spends their time across industry topics (journalist record). Bars are sorted by total participation descending.


Pro/Against Vote Direction — Per Member

Pro/Against stacked bar — vote direction (pro, against, neutral) per member. Individual member behaviour is consistent across datasets; only which discussions are included changes.

title: Finding 2: The Full Committee Is Balanced
text:
Network Analysis (Gephi)
Bipartite member–topic networks were built in Gephi to visualise the structural differences between datasets. Nodes represent members and topics; edges are weighted by participation count.

Gephi bipartite network — FILAH dataset. Members (circles) connected to topics (squares). Only fishing-adjacent topics and 3 members present.

Gephi bipartite network — Journalist dataset. All 6 members connected to all 15 topics. Balanced structure visible.

Gephi bipartite network — TROUT dataset. Only tourism-adjacent topics and 3 members present. Mirror image of FILAH.
The network graphs make the lobbies’ editorial choices structurally visible: each lobby’s network is a sparse, industry-skewed subgraph of the journalist’s dense, balanced network.

title: Finding 2: The Full Committee Is Balanced
text:
Key Takeaway
The full committee is balanced. COOTEFOO’s six members collectively represent both fishing and tourism interests in near-equal measure. The impression of bias only arises when the record is selectively filtered — as both lobbies did.
